skill.name: conductor-revert
skill.description: Guides safe git revert workflows for Conductor tracks, phases, and tasks with confirmation and verification steps.
skill.summary: Revert Conductor work units with git-aware planning, confirmation, and status updates.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many shell command patterns, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance indicators. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance hits are false positives from markdown text. The git command examples are legitimate but can modify repository state. No prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git status, git log searches, shell grep pipelines, and git revert commands. This fits a revert workflow. These commands can create revert commits and alter a user repository. Users need explicit review and confirmation before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown directly in the skill and are semantically necessary for git-aware reverts. The risk is real repository mutation, not evidence of malicious command execution.

medium_findings.title: Workflow File Modification Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to edit plan.md, metadata.json, and tracks.md after successful reverts. These changes are part of the documented workflow, but incorrect target selection could reset task status or metadata unexpectedly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem modification instructions are explicit and tied to Conductor state files. The skill includes confirmation and conflict handling, which lowers the severity.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Flags
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptography at lines 3 and 156, but these lines contain descriptive markdown text and a plan placeholder. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API use appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms these are plain documentation lines, not cryptographic code. This strongly supports a false-positive assessment.

low_findings.title: False Positive Reconnaissance Flags
low_findings.description: The scanner reported system and network reconnaissance on lines 5, 53, and 54. These lines are an argument hint and checks for merge or rebase state, with no host, network, or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text does not request network probing or system discovery. The flags appear to be keyword matches without hostile context.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git Command Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides shell command templates that include user-selected track, phase, and task placeholders. Agents should quote arguments and validate selectors before running commands in a real repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholders are visible in shell examples and could be substituted from user input. The file is documentation rather than executable code, so the injection risk depends on agent implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive-Looking Undo Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The undo section mentions reset and checkout commands as an alternative if not yet pushed. The surrounding text marks this as cautionary, but these commands can discard revert commits and working tree changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented with a caution and are not the primary path. They still represent a meaningful safety risk if followed without understanding the repository state.

content.value_statement: Undoing a Conductor task, phase, or track can be risky when commits and planning files are mixed together. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a reviewed git revert plan with confirmation and verification.
actual_capabilities: Selects a Conductor track, phase, or task as the revert target.
Finds related commits using git log searches and track identifiers.
Builds a revert plan that lists commits, files, and task status changes.
Requires an explicit YES confirmation before revert execution.
Guides conflict handling by halting and asking for human resolution.
Updates Conductor plan and track status after successful reverts.
limitations: It depends on consistent commit messages and Conductor track identifiers.
It cannot guarantee that every related commit will be discovered.
It does not resolve merge conflicts automatically.
It requires a git repository and existing Conductor tracking files.

use_cases.title: Undo a Completed Task
use_cases.description: Revert one finished Conductor task while preserving later review of the git history.

use_cases.title: Roll Back a Phase
use_cases.description: Remove a problematic phase and reset its tasks to pending before redesign work begins.

use_cases.title: Recover a Track Safely
use_cases.description: Plan a full track revert with affected files, commits, and verification steps visible before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Revert Target
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose which Conductor task, phase, or track to revert. Show the available options and explain the likely impact.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Task Revert
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a revert plan for task 2.3 in my Conductor track. Include matching commits, affected files, and the confirmation step.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Phase Rollback
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze phase 2 of this Conductor track and build a safe rollback plan. Do not run the revert until I confirm with YES.

prompt_templates.title: Execute and Verify a Track Revert
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide a full Conductor track revert from pre-flight checks through verification. Halt on conflicts and preserve git history.

output_examples.output: The assistant identifies matching commits, lists affected files, shows task status changes, and asks for explicit YES confirmation before any revert.

output_examples.input: A phase revert creates a merge conflict.
output_examples.output: The assistant stops the sequence, reports the conflicted files, and offers options to inspect, abort, or open manual resolution guidance.

output_examples.input: Undo a full track that was already pushed.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that new revert commits are safer than force pushing, then asks for confirmation before continuing.

best_practices: Review the full revert plan before approving any repository changes.
Use explicit track, phase, and task identifiers to avoid accidental broad reverts.
Run tests and inspect Conductor status after every successful revert.
anti_patterns: Do not approve a revert without checking the listed commits and affected files.
Do not use this skill to rewrite shared history or force push changes.
Do not continue automatically after merge conflicts or failed git commands.

faq.question: Does this skill use git reset by default?
faq.answer: No. The main workflow uses git revert to preserve history and avoid rewriting shared branches.

faq.question: Can it revert a single Conductor task?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports task, phase, and full track targets when commit messages include the relevant identifiers.

faq.question: Will it run without confirmation?
faq.answer: No. The instructions require the exact YES response before executing revert operations.

faq.question: Does it resolve merge conflicts?
faq.answer: No. It halts on conflicts and asks for human review before any continuation.

faq.question: Does it update Conductor planning files?
faq.answer: Yes. After successful reverts, it guides updates to task markers, metadata, and track status.

faq.question: Is this safe for pushed commits?
faq.answer: It recommends new revert commits instead of force pushes, which is safer for shared repositories.
